title: First Peoples establish enduring cultures
summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples develop the world’s oldest continuing cultures.
what: Hundreds of nations created distinct languages, laws, economies and relationships with Country.
why: Australian history cannot begin in 1788 without reproducing the logic of terra nullius.
colle: How should a settler nation rewrite its founding story?

title: Cook claims the east coast for Britain
summary: British imperial claims precede settlement.
what: James Cook mapped the east coast and claimed it for Britain as New South Wales.
why: The claim ignored existing sovereignty and prepared the way for colonisation.
colle: Can exploration be separated from conquest?

title: The First Fleet
summary: A penal colony is established at Sydney Cove.
what: British convicts, marines and officials arrived and founded New South Wales.
why: The event is celebrated as national origins by some and remembered as invasion by many Indigenous Australians.
colle: Should Australia Day remain on 26 January?

title: The gold rushes
summary: Migration and wealth transform the colonies.
what: Gold discoveries attracted migrants from around the world and accelerated urban growth.
why: The period promoted social mobility but also racism, environmental damage and conflict over immigration.
colle: Did the gold rush create a more democratic society?

title: The Eureka Stockade
summary: Miners rebel against colonial authority.
what: Gold miners fought troops after demanding fair licences and political representation.
why: Eureka became a flexible national symbol claimed by democrats, labour activists and nationalists.
colle: Why can one rebellion support very different political myths?

title: Federation and White Australia
summary: The Commonwealth is created alongside racial exclusion.
what: Six colonies federated, while the Immigration Restriction Act became the foundation of the White Australia policy.
why: The new democracy combined advanced political institutions with an explicitly racial vision of nationhood.
colle: Was racism built into the Australian nation?

title: Gallipoli and ANZAC
summary: Military defeat becomes a founding national myth.
what: Australian and New Zealand forces suffered heavy losses in the failed Gallipoli campaign.
why: ANZAC remembrance celebrates sacrifice and mateship but can overshadow Indigenous history and other wartime experiences.
colle: Does ANZAC unite Australia or narrow its national story?

title: The fall of Singapore and the turn to America
summary: Britain can no longer guarantee Australia’s security.
what: Japan captured Singapore and Australia increasingly relied on the United States during the Pacific War.
why: The strategic pivot reshaped foreign policy while Britain remained culturally important.
colle: Is Australia too dependent on the United States?

title: Federal voting rights for Indigenous Australians
summary: Remaining federal exclusions are removed.
what: Legislation gave all Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults the option to enrol and vote in federal elections.
why: Formal political inclusion advanced, though enrolment did not become compulsory for Indigenous citizens until later.
colle: Does formal equality guarantee political power?

title: The 1967 referendum
summary: A huge majority approves constitutional change concerning Indigenous Australians.
what: Voters allowed the Commonwealth to make laws for Aboriginal people and removed their exclusion from population counts.
why: The referendum became a symbol of recognition, although it did not grant citizenship or voting rights.
colle: Why do national myths sometimes simplify legal change?

title: The White Australia policy ends
summary: Immigration policy is formally made non-racial.
what: The Whitlam government completed the dismantling of discriminatory selection rules and promoted multiculturalism.
why: The change transformed Australia’s population and its relationship with Asia.
colle: Has multiculturalism replaced Australia’s racial past?

title: The Dismissal
summary: The Governor-General removes the Prime Minister.
what: John Kerr dismissed Gough Whitlam during a constitutional crisis over supply.
why: The event demonstrated that the Crown’s reserve powers can still have decisive political effects.
colle: Should Australia replace the monarchy with a republic?

title: The Australia Act
summary: Remaining constitutional links with Britain are ended.
what: Australian and British legislation removed the UK Parliament’s final powers over Australian law and appeals.
why: It completed legal independence without changing the monarchy.
colle: Can a country be fully independent while sharing a monarch?

title: The Mabo decision
summary: The High Court rejects terra nullius.
what: The Court recognised native title and the Meriam people’s continuing connection to land.
why: Mabo transformed property law and forced the country to confront the legal fiction behind colonisation.
colle: Can courts repair historical injustice?

title: Port Arthur and national gun reform
summary: Mass murder produces rapid bipartisan legislation.
what: After 35 people were killed in Tasmania, governments agreed to strict firearms controls and a buyback.
why: The response is often cited internationally as evidence that political consensus can reduce gun violence.
colle: Why was Australia able to adopt gun reform when the US has not?

title: The republic referendum
summary: Australians vote to retain the monarchy.
what: The proposed model for a president appointed by Parliament was rejected by 54.9% of voters.
why: The result showed that support for a republic does not automatically produce agreement on its form.
colle: Could a future republic referendum succeed?

title: The National Apology
summary: The Prime Minister apologises to the Stolen Generations.
what: Kevin Rudd acknowledged policies that removed Indigenous children from families.
why: The apology was a landmark in recognition, but gaps in health, education, incarceration and life expectancy persisted.
colle: Can symbolic apology matter without material repair?

title: Marriage equality
summary: Parliament legalises same-sex marriage after a postal survey.
what: A national survey returned a clear Yes vote and legislation followed.
why: The episode showed both rapid social liberalisation and controversy over using a non-binding public vote for minority rights.
colle: Should minority rights be put to a popular vote?

title: The Voice referendum
summary: Voters reject an Indigenous Voice to Parliament.
what: The proposed constitutional advisory body failed nationally and in every state.
why: The result exposed uncertainty, misinformation and deep disagreement over recognition and practical reform.
colle: Why did the Voice referendum fail?

title: Labor wins a second term with an enlarged majority
summary: Anthony Albanese is returned decisively.
what: The Australian Labor Party won 94 of 150 House seats in the federal election.
why: The result strengthened Labor while cost of living, housing, climate policy and the future of the Coalition remained central debates.
colle: Does a large parliamentary majority make bold reform more likely?
